title: Disabling 2FA
content:
Why Clients Request 2FA Disable

Two-Factor Authentication adds a critical layer of security, but there are legitimate situations where it must be temporarily disabled. The most common scenarios agents will encounter:

  • Lost or replaced device: The client's phone was lost, stolen, or factory-reset, and they no longer have access to their authenticator app.
  • App accidentally deleted: The authenticator app was removed, taking the Tklesson account entry with it.
  • Recovery codes lost: The client cannot locate their backup recovery codes and has no other way to log in.
  • Account locked: Multiple failed 2FA attempts have prevented access.

Important: Disabling 2FA removes the account's strongest protection. The account is then secured only by a password. Agents must complete identity verification before proceeding — never skip this step.

Identity Verification — Required Before Any Action

Use the same three-question verification as a password reset:

  1. What is your full name?
  2. What is your personal phone number on file?
  3. What are the last four digits of a payment method used on our platform?

If no payment method exists on file, skip the third question. If the client cannot answer these correctly, do not proceed — escalate to the Lead.

Disabling 2FA — Procedure

  1. Navigate to User Lookup and search for the client by name or email.
  2. Click their email address to open User Management.
  3. Verify the phone number and account status on screen match what the client provided.
  4. Click Disable 2FA and confirm the action.

The system clears the client's 2FA secret key and all backup codes immediately. They can now log in with only email and password.

After Disabling — Re-enrollment

Once the client confirms they've regained access, they must re-enable 2FA immediately. Walk them through setup or refer them to the Setting Up 2FA article. Key points to emphasize:

  • Without 2FA, the account is vulnerable to password-based attacks.
  • They should generate and save new recovery codes during re-enrollment — old codes are invalid after a disable.
  • If they lost their previous device, recommend Authy with cloud backup enabled to prevent this situation from recurring.

Key Talking Points for Agents

  • "I'll need to verify your identity before I can disable 2FA. This is to protect your account."
  • "Once I disable 2FA, you'll be able to log in with just your password. But we should set it back up right away."
  • "Save your new recovery codes somewhere safe — a password manager works great."
  • "If you ever lose access again, just contact us. We're here to help."

Confirm the client can log in successfully before closing the conversation.
